facts: A circuit needs a complete loop from one end of the battery, through wires and a device (like a light bulb), back to the other end of the battery, or else electricity cannot flow.
Magnets have two poles, north and south; opposite poles attract each other, but like poles (north-north or south-south) push each other away.
Electricity can be dangerous; you should never put metal objects or fingers into electrical outlets because the electricity can travel through your body and hurt you.
A switch is a part of a circuit that can open or close the path; when the switch is open, the circuit is broken and the device stops working.
misconceptions: Students think that a light bulb will light if only one wire connects it to a battery, because they believe electricity just flows out of the battery into the bulb, but a complete circuit is needed for electricity to flow.
Students believe that all magnets attract each other, so putting two magnets with the same poles together will make them stick, but like poles actually repel and push apart.
